items.title: I know there is some overlap between open source and anti-AI activists, but I have a hard time recon...
items.content:
I know there is some overlap between open source and anti-AI activists, but I have a hard time reconciling it. My million+ open source LOC were always intended as a gift to the world. Yes, I would make arguments about how it would strengthen our communities, and the GPL would prevent outright exploitation by our competitors, but those were to allay fears of my partners to allow me to make the gift.

AI training on the code magnifies the value of the gift. I am enthusiastic about it!

Some people do look at open source as a tool for social change, career advancement, or reputation building, but those are all downstream of the gift.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack writes a thoughtful essay arguing that open source code being used for AI training magnifies the value of the original gift to the world. He frames open source as fundamentally a gift, and argues that AI training on it extends that gift's value rather than exploiting it.
items.reasoning: This is the standout post in this batch. Carmack (legendary programmer, former CTO of Oculus) makes a significant philosophical argument about open source and AI training - one of the most contentious debates in tech. Very high engagement (275K views, 2.8K likes, 282 RTs). Original, thoughtful, from a uniquely credible voice.

items.content_html:
I know there is some overlap between open source and anti-AI activists, but I have a hard time reconciling it. My million+ open source LOC were always intended as a gift to the world. Yes, I would make arguments about how it would strengthen our communities, and the GPL would prevent outright exploitation by our competitors, but those were to allay fears of my partners to allow me to make the gift.

items.title: If you build an automation machine, the way to monetize it is to sell it to as many people as possib...
items.content:
If you build an automation machine, the way to monetize it is to sell it to as many people as possible -- anyone who has tasks to automate.

But if what you build is an invention machine, then the best way to monetize it is to use it yourself.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet draws a distinction between AI as automation (sell widely) vs. AI as invention (use yourself), arguing the monetization strategy should differ fundamentally.
items.reasoning: Highly insightful strategic framework from a very credible AI researcher. Massive engagement (1,398 likes, 101 RTs, 63K views). Thought-provoking distinction with major implications for AI business models.

This is a significant milestone for us. Alongside Finance, Defense and Intelligence is now a primary core focus area for our company.

We are building an integrated system to analyze massive amounts of multimodal data across land, sea, and air domains. By leveraging our autonomous AI agents and Small Vision-Language Models (SVLM) designed for high-speed processing on edge devices like drones, we aim to automate the pipeline from raw data observation to information integration.

National security relies heavily on information superiority. As a Japan-based AI company, we are proud to establish a dedicated domestic team of experts to ensure the secure implementation of our research. We are officially scaling our commitment to strengthening Japan's security infrastructure through Japan’s technological autonomy.

Autonomous AI agents just got a multi-year defense contract in Japan.

Sakana AI landed a contract f...
items.content:
Autonomous AI agents just got a multi-year defense contract in Japan.

Sakana AI landed a contract from Japan's Ministry of Defense to build an AI-powered Command and Control system.

> Multi-domain coverage: land, sea, air
> Raw drone data to decisions, fully automated
> Small Vision-Language Models running on-device
> No cloud dependency in critical environments

The core bet is on Small Vision-Language Models (SVLMs): compact models that run directly on drones and edge hardware, processing images and sensor data locally without sending anything to a server.

Multiple autonomous agents then fuse that distributed data into a single operational picture.

Before this, battlefield awareness required massive infrastructure and human analysts in the loop. Now a swarm of drones can observe, classify, and route decisions end-to-end, in real time, on the hardware already flying.

https://t.co/nFqjyCjTGU

items.title: Existing "OCR" technology for digitalizing PDFs has been around for ~30 years. Reading printed chara...
items.content:
Existing "OCR" technology for digitalizing PDFs has been around for ~30 years. Reading printed characters on a page and converting them into meaningful representations is a hard problem! 

Existing approaches were either dependent on pattern matching to specific document templates, or on specialized ML models for specific data distributions. They constantly needed template/model refitting and broke on the long-tail of varied docs.

Today, vision models are capable of much higher general accuracy without constant retraining, but they still need careful orchestration to make sure that they're able to attend to specific elements (tables, charts), and output semantically correct outputs. 

Our OCR platform LlamaParse is built on this "agentic OCR" foundation. A network of specialized agents will parse apart even the most complicated documents and reconstruct the outputs in a semantically meaningful way. We're excited to reach a world where raw parsing accuracy is not just 80% over "easy" docs, but 100% accurate over literally any document that exists.

Check it out: https://t.co/FeOoTjeKjf
LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) discusses the evolution of OCR from pattern matching to vision model-based 'agentic OCR', promoting LlamaParse as a platform using specialized agent networks to parse complex documents with higher accuracy.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical content from LlamaIndex CEO about a real product advancement. Good engagement (166 likes, 20K views). Explains the progression from traditional OCR to agentic approaches with vision models.

items.title:
Anthropic just made 1M-token context the new baseline for frontier models.

Claude Opus 4.6 now ship...
items.content:
Anthropic just made 1M-token context the new baseline for frontier models.

Claude Opus 4.6 now ships with a 1 million token context window, generally available on all plans and on Claude Code by default.

> 78.3% on MRCR v2 at 1M tokens, #1 among frontier models
> Previous version scored 18.5% on the same test
> 600 images or PDF pages per single request
> Beats competitors at the 1M token recall mark

MRCR v2 is a memory stress test. 8 specific facts are hidden inside 1M tokens of text. The model must find and return the exact right one, not just any similar match.

Most models collapse past 128K. Opus 4.6 holds.

Before this, loading a full codebase, a legal document set, or a multi-day agent session into one context meant chunking, summarizing, or losing information.

Now you send it all at once, and the model actually remembers what's in it.
https://t.co/EHTso4ex2k

OpenAI just silently patched a bug that was degrading GPT-5.4's vision.

The image encoder, the comp...
items.content:
OpenAI just silently patched a bug that was degrading GPT-5.4's vision.

The image encoder, the component that converts pixels into tokens the model can reason over, had a small bug affecting image inputs.

OpenAI quietly shipped a fix. No redeployment needed on your end.

Here's what this touches:
> Document and chart parsing accuracy
> High-res image localization
> Computer-use click precision
> OCR on dense scanned forms

items.content_html:
@sangwan_ri34441 @andriy_mulyar @nomic_ai @adityakusupati MRL is the worst possible compression method. Only use it when your vector DB doesn't support other compression methods.

Not all embedding models are suitable for binarization. Many of them drop significantly.

Cohere Embed v4 was trained with compression awareness: Binary (32x compression) keeps 90-95% of the original performance, esp. when you keep queries in float. For higher compressions use PQ

items.title:
The tiny corp just announced a GPU that ships in a shipping container.

The tiny corp makes tinybox,...
items.content:
The tiny corp just announced a GPU that ships in a shipping container.

The tiny corp makes tinybox, a multi-GPU AI training box. The exabox is their next product, arriving in 2027.

> Fits inside a shipping container
> Controlled from a Python notebook
> Behaves as one single massive GPU
> Confirmed to ship, specs may still change

The trick is tinygrad, their open-source ML framework. It abstracts all the hardware complexity away, so dozens of physical GPUs look like one unified device to your code.

Before this, running a distributed GPU cluster meant wrestling with networking code, manual memory management, and multi-process orchestration.

Now you write a Python script, point it at the exabox, and it handles the rest. Your agent can do the same.

Local compute at a scale that was only possible inside hyperscaler data centers just became something you can own.

https://t.co/EswHlmM8mK

Perplexity just launched Computer o...
items.content:
Your AI agent can now run multi-hour workflows from your phone.

Perplexity just launched Computer on iOS, a cloud-based agentic system with full cross-device sync.

> Orchestrates ~20 specialized AI models
> Books travel, fills forms, writes code
> Tasks run for hours, even without you
> Start on phone, finish on desktop

Here's the key mechanic: you describe an outcome in plain language. The system breaks it into subtasks, then routes each one to the model best suited for that job.

One agent drafts a document while another pulls live data to fill it. All cloud-side, no local setup needed.

Before this, spinning up a multi-agent workflow meant either staying at your desktop or losing the thread entirely when switching devices.

Now the full orchestration layer sits in your pocket. You can trigger a complex, multi-step pipeline from anywhere, and it keeps running whether your screen is on or not.

https://t.co/EtlUYu8mHj

items.title:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal: Must-read papers on diffusion language models

Diffusion language m...
items.content:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal: Must-read papers on diffusion language models

Diffusion language models represent a fundamental reimagining of how machines generate text — moving away from the autoregressive, left-to-right token prediction that has dominated the field, toward an iterative denoising process borrowed from continuous diffusion in vision. 

Rather than committing to each word sequentially, these models learn to refine entire sequences from noise, enabling parallel generation, flexible conditioning, and novel forms of control over the output.

Though the paradigm originated in image synthesis, adapting it to the discrete, structured nature of language has required deep innovations in noise schedules, representation, and training objectives — innovations that are still actively unfolding.

By reading the papers of this curriculum, learners will master the foundational theories, innovative architectures, and critical techniques of diffusion language models, enabling them to master and advance the science of artificial language generation from first principles.

items.title:
I can't wait for the day we don't need to hear "your call is important to us" ever again.

Literally...
items.content:
I can't wait for the day we don't need to hear "your call is important to us" ever again.

Literally nobody wants to talk to a bot on the phone, but we all hate waiting in line.

Here is the thing about voice:

We've been talking to each other for thousands of years, but we've only been typing for maybe 50.

Voice is the most natural interface humans have.

But voice AI still sounds robotic and awkward, with zero emotional awareness.

If we can unlock voice AI at scale and address all of these issues, we'll get access to the most natural and powerful interface we've ever had.

Here is the good news:

I'm working with ElevenLabs, and they released a platform that lets you build and deploy voice and chat agents that sound really good.

These voice agents are:

1. Real-time
2. Multilingual
3. With emotional inflection

They work so much better. They don't make people hang up the phone immediately!

What really matters here is that these agents can handle thousands of simultaneous conversations without making you wait through hold music.

They integrate with existing systems, resolve issues, qualify leads, and escalate with full context when a human needs to step in.

ElevenLabs got this right.

items.content:
Putting them side by side in X Pro you can see the full feed. They go fast. 

But it also lets you see what the algorithm picks (and helps the algorithm give you more of what you want). 

If you feed more quality people and companies in the top, the algo has more to pick from for your feed. 

My project that will turn on next week helps everyone see what's going on in AI bubbles, but won't help you see other bubbles. But it gives a taste of how you can really "monitor the situation" in a whole new way.

@redhairshanks86 Yes for sure

People who do whatever they want are a threat to NPCs until the trends they start become mainstream

I got lots of hate from NPCs a decade ago going digital nomad and promoting it with my sites and then suddenly it became mainstream ~2020

Same with @bryan_johnson's health protocol, first he gets hated then NPCs do same 10y later

Same will happen with stuff Clavicular does etc

NPCs are anxious and always feel threatened because they weren't able to do what they really want

My mon and dad really taught me to just do whatever I want as long as it makes me happy

More important than following the crowd, but difficult!

items.title:
I was never able to do blood tests when I asked for it in Netherlands

Doctor asked "why? you're not...
items.content:
I was never able to do blood tests when I asked for it in Netherlands

Doctor asked "why? you're not sick?"

Then I tried in Portugal (at Germano de Sousa) but they never picked up the phone or when they did were so slow and unhelpful I gave up, they also require a doctor prescription btw

The first place I could get my blood tested was Thailand in 2018, I just walked into Bumrungrad and asked for it, amazing experience

Last few years we just fly to Brazil and do it here, the nurse comes to your home/hotel at 8am and takes your blood, same or next day results online

I find it funny I keep having to fly out of Western Europe to do blood tests, they make it impossibly hard to do them

Which is retarded
